segments.s223-g33: بلشکر is a vocative address to the host/troops (here used as 'O army'); نگه کن is an imperative phrase where نگه is a noun/verb 'watch, guard' and کن is the imperative 'do' attached to it. ز introduces the object or source of caution ('against' / 'from') in a poetic compressed form, and کارآگهان is a plural/collective noun 'the aware/informed (ones)'—i.e., watch out for those who know/spy.
segments.s223-g34: کسی here means 'someone/anyone' and is followed by the archaic/cohortive particle کو (used like a relative 'who/that' in older Persian). سخن باز جوید is a verbal phrase: سخن 'speech/words' + باز 'open / openly' and جوید (3rd-person sing. present) 'seeks/tries', so 'who seeks to open/speak words'; نهان qualifies the action as concerning the hidden/secret—'secretly' or 'hidden'.
segments.s223-g35: ببیند is a 3rd-person-present/subjunctive-form used here optatively/imperatively ('let them see' / 'may they see'); که introduces the content/relative clause 'who...'; این شیر دل مرد is a compound epithet: این 'this', شیر 'lion' plus دل 'heart' forming 'lion‑hearted', and مرد here means 'man/hero'—کیست is the copula question 'who is (he)'.
segments.s223-g36: وزین لشکر is a descriptive noun phrase: وزین 'weighty / formidable' modifying لشکر 'army/host'. او را is the object (او 'him', را the definite-object marker). هم آورد is a verb phrase: هم 'also/too' + آورد 'brought', and the clause ends with the interrogative کیست 'who is (the one)'. The whole asks 'and who is it that also brought him to this mighty army?'
segments.s223-g37: از آن پس is a temporal prepositional phrase meaning “from that time on” (از = from; آن پس = then/after). همه تن is the direct object phrase ‘every person’ (همه = all/every; تن = person/body). بکشتن دهیم is an older/periphrastic verb construction: the infinitive بکشتن (to kill) precedes the finite verb دهیم (1st-person-plural), so together it means ‘we will cause/have (them) killed’ or more loosely ‘we will kill/put to death’.
segments.s223-g38: به آوردگه is a directional phrase ‘to the assembly/venue’ (به = to; آوردگه = place of bringing/assembly). بر سر و تن نهیم contains بر (on/upon) plus the coordinated objects سر و تن (‘head and body’). نهیم is the 1st-person-plural form of نهادن (to place/set), here continuing the action of the previous clause: ‘we will place/set (their) head and body (there).’
segments.s223-g39: بپیران is a dative-like phrase ‘to the elders’ (بـ = to/for + پیران = elders). چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’. گفت is the past tense verb ‘said/spoke’. خاقان چین is the subject ‘the Khaghan of China’ placed after the verb, a common classical Persian word order (verb before subject) that emphasizes the addressed party first.
segments.s223-g40: که introduces the reported content (‘that’). خود is an intensifier here meaning ‘itself’ or ‘the matter itself’. درد ازینست is a copular statement: درد (trouble/pain) + ازینست (archaic contracted form of از این است) = ‘the trouble comes from this’ or ‘this is the cause’. و تیمار ازین parallels the previous clause with تیمار (care/remedy) and the shortened ازین (‘from this’), so: ‘and the care/remedy is from this.’
segments.s223-g25: سزد‌گر is a formulaic conditional/adverbial meaning 'if it is fitting' or 'should it be proper'. It introduces the clause that follows. سر پیل را is a definite object phrase (سر 'head' + پیل 'elephant' + را object marker), and روز کین is a temporal/genitive phrase 'the day of vengeance' placed after the object for emphasis (typical flexible poetic order).
segments.s223-g26: This sequence gives the actions: بگیرد is a jussive/subjunctive-style 3rd-person verb 'he should seize / he takes' tied to the conditional opened by سزد‌گر; برآرد is another 3rd-person verb 'he lifts up / brings up' (literally 'brings upon'); زند here is the 3rd-person singular of زدن 'to strike', followed by بر زمین 'onto (the) ground' (بر + زمین). The verbs are coordinated to show consecutive actions: seize, raise, and strike down.
segments.s223-g27: سپه 'army' is the subject, سربسر 'completely / entirely' is an adverb modifying the verb, پیش خاقان 'before the khagan' is a prepositional phrase (پیش = in the presence of). شدند is the 3rd-person plural past/resultative 'they became / they came' used here to mean 'they came/assembled'. Word order places adverbial emphasis before the verb, typical of poetic usage.
segments.s223-g28: ز here has a causal/relational sense ('for/because of' or 'with regard to') introducing the reason or object of sorrow; کاموس is the proper name (not the common noun senses listed) and thus does not match the supplied dictionary senses; با درد و گریان شدند is a compound predicate 'they were with sorrow and became weeping' where با درد = 'with grief' and گریان شدند = 'they wept/they were crying'.
segments.s223-g29: که is the conjunction/complementizer 'that' introducing the clause; آغاز و فرجام is a noun pair 'the beginning and the end/outcome'; این رزمگاه is the noun phrase 'this battlefield' modifying that pair. The order is standard: conjunction + nouns + demonstrative + noun.
segments.s223-g30: شنیدی و دیدی are 2nd-person singular perfect/past verbs 'you heard and you saw'. بنزد سپاه ('in the presence of the army') is a prepositional/adverbial phrase with بنزد = 'before/near to' and سپاه = 'army'; it specifies where this hearing and seeing took place.
segments.s223-g31: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. چاره کار ما is a genitive phrase 'the remedy of our affair(s)' (چاره = remedy; کار ما = our affair). بازجوی here functions as an imperative 'seek/inquire after' directed to the addressee; in classical/poetic Persian the verb can follow the object for emphasis (object then verb).
segments.s223-g32: بتنها 'only/alone' restricts the following phrase. تن خویش is the reflexive possessive 'your own self' (تن = body/self; خویش = own — here split orthographically as خوی + ش). و کس را مگوی is a negative imperative: مگوی 'do not tell' (2nd-person sing. negative), کس را 'to anyone' (کس = anyone/someone with را marking the object). The clause advises confidentiality.
segments.s223-g1: کنون‌ای is the vocative opening: کنون (now) plus the vocative ای attached to call someone. خردمند روشن‌روان is a vocative epithet: خردمند (wise) + روشن‌روان (literally “bright‑souled,” an epithet), placed after the vocative marker. The whole segment addresses someone directly and sets the temporal frame.
segments.s223-g2: بجز نام یزدان is an exception phrase: بَجز (except) + نام (name) + یزدان (God). مگردان زبان is a negative imperative: مگردان = “do not turn/turn not” (poetic negative imperative), followed by زبان (tongue/speech) as the object (your tongue, implied possessive). Together: "do not turn your tongue to anything except the name of God."
segments.s223-g3: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause (“for/that”). اویست is a poetic form corresponding to اوست (“he/it is”) — here used predicatively (note: the provided lemma senses list seems to treat اویست as a proper name, but in context it functions as the copula + pronoun). بر نیک و بد means “over/concerning good and evil” (بر = over/toward, نیک = good, بد = bad). رهنمای is a noun meaning “guide.” The clause says “for he/it is guide over good and evil.”
segments.s223-g4: وزویست is the contracted phrase وز وی است = و ز وی است (“and from/through him it is”), here indicating agency/source. گردون گردان combines گردون (the sky/heavens) with گردان (revolving/that turns); grammatically گردان functions as an adjective/participle describing the heavens. بجای (= به جای) means “in its place” or “in the appointed place.” The whole segment means roughly “and by Him the revolving heavens are placed in their stead.”
segments.s223-g5: همی + بگذرد is an optative/hortative construction: همی (an emphatic/continuous particle) with بگذرد (3rd‑person subjunctive/optative of گذر — “may it pass”) gives a wish: “may it always pass.” بر تو (upon you) is the beneficiary/experiencer, and ایام تو (your days) is the grammatical subject placed after the verb for poetic word‑order: “may your days pass over you.”
segments.s223-g6: سرایی here is a noun ‘a dwelling/house’ (derived from the verb سراییدن), not the verb sense. جزین (= جز این) is the exception phrase “except this.” باشد is the subjunctive/optative third person of بودن used as a wish (“may there be”), and آرام تو means “your peace/quiet” (آرام as adjective/noun + تو). Put together: “may there be no dwelling for you except this peaceful one.”
segments.s223-g7: چو باشی is a conditional/temporal opener: چو = چو/چون (“when/if”) + باشی (2nd‑person subjunctive/present of بودن) meaning “if you are” or “when you are.” بدین گفته is بدین = به + این (“to/with this”) + گفته (“saying/statement”), so بدین گفته means “with/on this saying” (i.e. “in agreement with this statement”). همداستان is an adjective meaning “of the same mind / in agreement.” The whole segment reads “if you are in agreement with this saying.” Note: the input list labels the part دِین, but here بدین is the compound 'به + این'.
segments.s223-g8: که introduces the following explanatory clause (“for/that”). دهقان in Shahnameh contexts often names the traditional storyteller/class of landed gentry (dihqān) who preserve ancient lore; here it means the dehqān/old narrator. همی is a habitual/emphatic particle (“always/ever”), and گوید is the verb “says” (the provided sense list seems to give noun senses; here it functions as the verb). از باستان = “from antiquity / since ancient times.” So the clause says “for the dehqān has always said it since ancient times.”
segments.s223-g9: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'thereupon/after that' (ازان = 'thereupon'; پس = 'after/then'). خبر شد is an impersonal verba phrase meaning 'it became known / news came' (خبر = 'news'; شد = 'happened/became'). بخاقان چین is written without a space but parses as به خاقانِ چین 'to the Khaghan (emperor) of China' (a recipient), an older/poetic rendering.
segments.s223-g10: که introduces a reported clause 'that'. شد here is the past intransitive 'happened / was (the case)'. کشته is a predicative past participle meaning 'killed/slain' (used adjectivally with شد). کاموس is a personal name (here not one of the listed noun senses). بر + دشت = 'on the plain'; کین qualifies the plain 'of vengeance' (دشت کین = 'Plain of Vengeance').
segments.s223-g11: This is a list of groups/names ('کشانی و شگنی و گردان بلخ'). In this poetic context these items function as subjects (names/ethnonyms or troop-names). گردان بلخ = 'the troop(s) of Balkh' (گردان = troop; بلخ = Balkh). The first two items are proper names/ethnic labels rather than the verbal senses listed.
segments.s223-g12: ز here is causal/poetic 'because of / on account of'. کاموس‌شان = 'their Kamus' (proper name + possessive). تیره شد روز و تلخ = 'the day became dark and bitter' (تیره = 'dark/ominous'; شد = simple past 'became'; روز = 'day'; تلخ = figurative 'bitter/harsh').
segments.s223-g13: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. بدیگر here means 'toward each other/one another' (directional use). نهادند is the past tense plural of نهادن 'to place/set' used here idiomatically with روی 'face'—نهادند روی = 'turned/placed their faces'. روی = 'face' (literal or idiomatic 'turned').
segments.s223-g14: که introduces an explanatory relative/clausal phrase 'for/that'. این = 'this/the foregoing'. پرهنر is an epithet 'well-skilled / accomplished'. مرد = 'man / person' (here 'man' as the hero). پرخاشجوی is an adjectival epithet 'quarrelsome / aggressive' describing that man.
segments.s223-g15: This is a string of questions. چه مردست = 'what sort of man is (he)' (مردست = contraction of مرد است 'is a man' in older/poetic spelling). و = 'and'. این مرد را نام چیست = a word order typical of Persian questions where را marks the topical/definite object: 'this man's name what is?' (نام = 'name'; چی = 'what'; ست = copula 'is').
segments.s223-g16: هم آورد او = 'he also brought/said/related' (هم = 'also'; آورد here in the sense 'report/relate'). در جهان مرد کیست = a rhetorical question 'who is this man in the world?' where کی = 'who' and ست = 'is' (inversion for emphasis). The whole segment reports that he then asked/reported this question about the man's identity.
segments.s223-g17: چنین (thus) is an adverb introducing reported speech; گفت is the past verb 'said' placed before the speaker (هومان) — a common poetic order. به + noun (پیران شیر) marks the indirect object 'to Pīrān-šīr' (an epithet/name), so the whole phrase reads 'Thus Houmān said to Pīrān-šīr.'
segments.s223-g18: که here introduces the content of what was said ('that' / introducing direct speech). امروز is the temporal adverb 'today/now'; شد is the past of شدن 'became' used here with a predicative phrase; جانم is جان + possessive suffix -م 'my life/soul'; از marks source/causal 'from/of'; رزم 'battle' and سیر 'sated' (noun/adjective). Word order is slightly inverted and elliptical: literally 'that today became my-soul from battle sated' → 'that today my soul has become sated with battle.'
segments.s223-g19: دلیران ما 'our brave ones' is the noun phrase (دلیران + possessive ما). چون here is comparative 'like/as' and introduces the simile; فرازند is 'offspring/sons' and چنگ is a name (poetic proper name). The sentence omits the copula (بودند) — a typical poetic ellipsis — so: 'Our brave men were like Chang's sons.'
segments.s223-g20: که links this clause to the previous idea ('so/and thus'); شد is past 'became' used with the past participle کشته to form a passive-like result 'was slain' (literally 'became killed'). کاموس here is a proper name (the warrior); جنگی is an adjective/noun 'warlike/warrior' modifying کاموس; بجنگ is a verbal/adverbial form 'in battle' or 'to battle' (the b- prefix gives a locative/infinitival nuance). Altogether: 'so Kamus the warrior was killed in battle.'
segments.s223-g21: بگیتی 'in the world' is an adverbial prepositional phrase; چنو (چنین/چنو) = 'such' qualifies the following noun; نامداری 'renown/fame'; نبود is the negative past 'there was not.' Literal: 'In the world there was no such renown.'
segments.s223-g22: این عبارت is compressed/elliptical: وزو is an archaic/contracted form (functioning like وز او 'than/from him') and here introduces the comparison or relation to the previous referent; پیلتن‌تر shows a comparative (-تر) built on پیلتن (a name/place) meaning 'more Pīltan-like' or 'greater than Pīltan'; سواری 'rider/horseman'; نبود 'there was not.' The clause means roughly 'nor was there a horseman more (like) Pīltan.'
segments.s223-g23: چو here = 'when'. کاموس گو is a compound/epithet (proper name plus گو as an epithet-forming element, '-go'/'-speaking' or part of a name); را is the definite object marker; بخم is an archaic/poetic word (marked here as obscure) providing a local/detail sense; کمند 'snare/lasso' is the instrument. The clause therefore says 'When Kamus-go fell into/took the snare...' (poetic phrasing and some words are archaic/elliptical).
segments.s223-g24: به آوردگه 'to the place of bringing/assembly' is a locative phrase (به + آوردگه). بر توان 'with strength/force' (بر + توان) indicates manner/means; کرد is the past verb 'did/made' and بند is the noun 'fetter/bond' (object) — together 'they made a fetter / bound (him) and brought him to the assembly.' Word order is verb-final within the clause and the phrase describes the action done after capture.
segments.s223-g57: چنین گفت is a formula meaning “thus he said” (چنین = thus; گفت = he said). کین مرد جنگی strings together modifiers: کین here functions as a demonstrative/pointing word (archaic) modifying مرد جنگی (“that warlike man”), with جنگی an adjectival form ‘warlike/fighter’. بتیر (written here as a single word) modifies مرد جنگی and indicates relation to arrows (literally ‘with/by arrow’), i.e. ‘arrow‑armed’ or ‘an archer’. Word order is head (مرد) then modifier (جنگی, بتیر) typical of older Persian compounding.
segments.s223-g58: This short nominal phrase lists epithets of the man: سوار = ‘rider/horseman’ (noun), کمندافگن = describing someone who casts a lasso (present participial compound), و = ‘and’, گردگیر = a dust‑cloak or cloak that keeps off dust. The three nouns are coordinated to give a string of qualities.
segments.s223-g59: نگه کرد باید is an older word order where the verbal noun نگه + کرد and the modal باید express necessity: ‘it is necessary to watch/keep watch’. The clause که جایش کجاست is a subordinate content clause introduced by که (‘that/so that’) with جای + possessive ش = ‘his place’ and کجاست the copular question ‘where is’. Overall: ‘one must watch to see where his place is.’
segments.s223-g60: بگرد is an exhortative/imperative form meaning ‘look/turn/search (around)’. The phrase that follows names the loci to be searched: چپ لشکر = ‘the left of the army’ and دست راست = ‘the right hand’ (i.e. the right flank). The coordination with و links the two search locations.
segments.s223-g61: هم ... هم ... is the correlative pair ‘both ... and ...’. از شهر پرسد means ‘(one) asks in/at the town’ or ‘inquire from the town’, with پرسد here the present/jussive form ‘(let him) ask’/‘asks’. هم از نام او adds the parallel object ‘and (ask) about his name’. The verb پرسد governs both complements.
segments.s223-g62: ازانپس is an adverb meaning ‘from then on / after that’. بسازیم is the 1st‑person‑plural subjunctive/hortative of ساختن used here as ‘let us make/arrange’ or ‘we will make/settle’. فرجام او = ‘his end / outcome / fate’ is the object: ‘after that we will settle his fate.’ Word order places the adverb first and the verb before its object.
segments.s223-g63: This is a noun phrase made of the head سواری ‘rider/horseman’ followed by two adjectival epithets: سرافراز ‘lofty / triumphant / honored’ and خسروپرست ‘king‑worshipping / a devotee of the sovereign’. The adjectives follow the head, a common poetic order to pile epithets.
segments.s223-g64: بیامد is simple past ‘he came / arrived’. زد برین کار دست is an idiomatic sequence: زد (strike/put) + برین (بر + این = ‘upon this’) + کار دست (‘the work / the matter in hand’), meaning ‘he laid his hand to this work’ or ‘he took the matter in hand’. The short word ببر in this line is unclear/ambiguous in isolation (it can mean ‘tiger’ or be an imperative ‘take!’); here the best reading is to treat the whole phrase as the idiom ‘(he) came and put his hand to this task’. Grammatically, زد takes برین کار دست as its object phrase.
segments.s223-g73: ازو is the archaic/clitic form of از او 'from/of him' placed before the verb; کین is a noun 'vengeance/hatred' and here functions as the object/goal of the action; کاموس in this line is a proper name (not the dictionary/ocean senses listed) and is therefore marked with -1 below; جویم (from جستن/جو) is first‑person singular present/subjunctive 'I seek' or 'I shall seek'; نخست is an adverb 'first' that modifies the whole verb phrase and appears at the end of the clause.
segments.s223-g74: پس از مرگ forms the temporal prepositional phrase 'after the death' (پس = 'after' here, plus the preposition از); نامش is the possessed noun 'his name/reputation' (نام + enclitic ش 'his'); بیارم is a first‑person verbal form of آوردن 'bring/restore' (here used in the sense 'I will bring/restore'), and درست is an adverb 'properly/rightly' modifying how the name will be brought/restored.
segments.s223-g75: برو is an interjection/imperative 'Go!' preserved as reported speech; آفرین together with کرد (آفرین کرد) is a light‑verb construction meaning 'uttered/said “bravo”/praised'; خاقان چین is the noun phrase 'the Khaghan of China' (title + place) and is the agent performing کرد.
segments.s223-g76: بپیشش is a locative/directional phrase 'before him / in his presence' (be‑ + پیش + enclitic ش); ببوسید is past third‑person 'he kissed'; چنگش is a possessed noun 'his cheng/hand/grip' (poetically used as the instrument or part used to kiss or touch) and زمین is the place 'the ground' — together they describe kissing the ground (with his hand/gesture) before that person.
segments.s223-g77: بدو گفت is the common sequence 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'); ار is the conditional 'if'; این کینه is 'this hatred/grudge' as the object of the conditional; بازآوری is the verbal noun/infinitival form 'revive/bring back' (here 'if you bring back/revive this hatred').
segments.s223-g78: سوی من is the directional phrase 'to me / toward me'; سر is the noun 'head' (here used idiomatically for a person or life presented to someone); بی‌نیاز is an adjective/adverb 'free of need / untroubled'; آوری (from آوردن) is second/third‑person present/subjunctive 'you bring' or 'you shall bring' — together: 'come to me and bring (your) head and you will be made free of want.'
segments.s223-g79: ببخشمت is a first‑person verb with enclitic object 'مت' meaning 'I grant/give to you' (a gifting/forgiveness verb); چندان governs the quantity 'so many' or 'to that extent' and modifies گهرها 'gems' (plural); ز گنج is the source phrase 'from the treasury/treasure'.
segments.s223-g80: کزان is a relative/ablative 'from which / from that' referring back to the treasure/gems; پس here means 'after/thereafter'; نباید is the impersonal/modal 'one should not / you need not'; کشیدنت is the verbal noun with enclitic 'ت' 'your being made to suffer' (from کشیدن 'to suffer' in this context); رنج is the noun 'suffering / hardship' — together: 'from which thereafter you should not have to endure suffering.'
segments.s223-g65: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') referring back to a person. چنگش is a possessive form (چنگ + -ش) literally 'his cheng' (here used metaphorically as 'his grasp/urge'); بدش is the adjective بد with the enclitic -ش ('bad to/for him') but in this poetic line functions idiomatically to indicate an inclination toward what follows (نام و جوینده). نام و جوینده is a coordinated noun phrase 'fame (reputation) and seeker' and بود is the past copula 'was', so the whole clause says that he 'was one whose craving/ambition was for fame and who sought it.'
segments.s223-g66: دلیر is a predicative adjective 'brave.' و links the two predicates. به هر کار + پوینده is a prepositional phrase where به marks relation ('in/at/with respect to'), هر کار means 'every deed/enterprise', and پوینده (present-participial adjective) means 'active/striving/moving' (i.e. industrious or eager in action). بود is the past copula 'was.' Together: 'and was brave and active in every undertaking.'
segments.s223-g67: بخاقان is a poetic prepositional phrase 'to the khaghan/king' (orthography joins به + خاقان); چنین گفت means 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here denotes 'said'); کای is the vocative particle 'O' and سرفراز is the vocative adjective 'exalted / victorious.' Grammatically this is a reporting clause followed by a direct address.
segments.s223-g68: جهان را uses را to mark/emphasize 'the world' as the (affected) topic. بمهر is a prepositional compound (ب + مهر) 'with love/affection.' تو is the second-person pronoun 'you'; بادا is the optative auxiliary 'may it be'; نیاز here functions as a noun meaning 'longing/adoration' or 'devotional regard.' The sequence expresses a wish: 'May the world, with love, be yours / may the world's affection be toward you.'
segments.s223-g69: گر is the conditional 'if.' او is the third-person pronoun 'he.' شیر جنگیست is a noun phrase with a fused copula: شیر 'lion' + جنگیست ('is warlike' or 'is a warrior'), i.e. 'is a war-lion/warrior.' بیجان is an adjective/adverb 'lifeless' and کنم is the first-person singular verb 'I will make' (here volitional/subjunctive meaning 'I will cause him to be lifeless'). The clause expresses a conditional threat: 'If he is a war-lion, I will make him lifeless.'
segments.s223-g70: بدانگه is a temporal/adverbial 'thereupon' or 'then.' که introduces the content clause that follows. سر سوی ایران کردن is an idiomatic verbal expression: سر ('head') + سوی ('toward') + ایران (proper noun) + کردن (کنم here 'I will turn/ direct'), so together 'I will turn my head toward Iran' / 'I will set out for Iran.' کنم is the first-person present/subjunctive form 'I will do/make.'
segments.s223-g71: بتنها is an adverbial 'alone/only.' تن خویش is a possessive noun phrase literally 'the body of his/own body' (خویش formed by خوی + -ش) used idiomatically for 'my own person/body.' جنگ‌آورم is a first-person present/past-stem form meaning 'I am a warrior' or 'I will fight/engage in war' (the -م ending marks first-person). Together: 'Alone, with my own body, I will fight.'
segments.s223-g72: همه is 'all' or 'entirely.' نام او is 'his name' where نام means reputation/fame and او is 'his.' زیر ننگ is a prepositional phrase 'under shame/disgrace.' آورم is the first-person singular verb 'I bring/cause' (here 'I will bring about / I will cause to be placed under'). The clause means 'I will bring all his name under shame,' i.e. 'I will disgrace his entire reputation.'
segments.s223-g41: که ... تا کیست is introducing a rhetorical question: که (interrogative particle here) + تا (until) + کی (when) + ست (archaic copula 'is'). زان is a demonstrative ('that' / 'from that') qualifying لشکر 'army/host', and پرگزند is an adjective 'full of harm' describing the host. Word order is typical of classical Persian: the temporal question ('تا کیست') comes before the descriptive phrase ('زان لشکر پرگزند').
segments.s223-g42: کجا (where) begins the second interrogative clause. پیل is the subject 'elephant'; گیرد is the third-person singular present/optative of گرفتن ('take, seize') used here in the sense 'be caught' or 'be seized'. بخم is an archaic/poetic locative/relational element meaning 'in the bend/curve', and کمند is 'snare/lasso' — together بخم کمند = 'in the snare's bend'. The clause therefore asks where an elephant will be trapped.
segments.s223-g43: ابا آنک introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('since/for' in classical usage); آنک is the relative/causal demonstrative 'that/for which'. از مرگ خود is an ablative/genitive phrase 'concerning one's death' (خود = 'one's own'). چاره نیست is a present-tense existential statement 'there is no remedy/means'. The verb نیست is the negative copula indicating absence.
segments.s223-g44: ره ('way, means') is the grammatical subject/topic: 'there is no way/means of ...'. The following nouns خواهش (request/plea), پرسش (question), and یاره (helper/attendant) are a coordinated list of things that lack existence here. The final نیست negates the whole list: 'there is no way for pleading, questioning, or for a helper.'
segments.s223-g45: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from'. مادر is the source 'mother'. همه ('all / everyone') modifies the following clause. مرگ را is marked with the direct-object/definite marker را but functions here as a destined state ('to death'); زاده‌ایم is the perfect first-person plural of زادن ('to be born') and thus means 'we have been born' or 'we were born'. The clause literally says we all have been born from (the) mother unto death.
segments.s223-g46: بناکام is an adverbial/adjectival qualifier 'unsuccessfully / in vain'. گردن ('neck') stands metonymically for life/submit (i.e., one's neck/life). بدو ('to it' / 'unto him/it') is the indirect object referring back to death, and داده‌ایم is the compound perfect 'we have given'. The literal sense: 'we have given our necks to it in vain.'
segments.s223-g47: کس here means 'no one' (a negative universal) and is the grammatical subject. از گردش آسمان is a genitive/ablative phrase 'beyond/from the circuit/revolution of the heavens'. نگذرد is the negative present/subjunctive form of گذشتن ('to pass'); together the clause states a general truth: 'no one passes beyond the sky's rotation.'
segments.s223-g48: وگر is a conditional ('but if / even if'). بر زمین is 'upon the earth', پیل را is the definite object 'the elephant' (را marks definiteness), and بشکرد is an archaic poetic verb meaning 'be broken/shattered' (here a past/subjunctive-like or counterfactual: 'were shattered'). The clause reinforces the impossibility in the previous clause: even if a great elephant were smashed to the ground, no one escapes the heavens' course.
segments.s223-g49: شما (you) is the addressee (second-person plural/polite). دل مدارید is a present/subjunctive-type clause meaning 'let the heart not be moved' or more idiomatically 'do not be moved in heart / do not pity'; مدارید is the verb 'مداریدن' (treat gently / be lenient) directed at 'دل' (heart). ازو is a short form meaning 'of him / for him' and links the verb to the person in question; مستمند is a noun/adjective 'needy, poor' identifying that person.
segments.s223-g50: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. کشته is the past participle 'killed/slain' and شد functions as the auxiliary 'was' (passive formation). زیر is the preposition 'under/beneath', خم here means 'bend/curve', and کمند is the lasso/snare that causes the killing; the phrase gives the location/means of the killing.
segments.s223-g51: مر here functions as an exclamatory/ethical particle ('woe to / alas for' lit. 'to me' in older poetry) followed by آن را که introducing a relative clause 'the one who...'. کاموس is a proper name (a person) and ازو indicates 'by him' or 'because of him'; شد هلاک is a verbal phrase 'became ruined / was destroyed' (شد as auxiliary forming the passive/result).
segments.s223-g52: ببند is the imperative 'bind / close' (an order). کمند is the direct object 'the lasso'. اندر is the preposition 'in/into', and آرم is a first-person present/future verb 'I rest / I will place' used here in the sense 'I will set/lay (him)'. بخاک is a directional phrase 'into the earth / to the ground / into dust' that completes the action.
segments.s223-g53: همه شهر ایران is a noun phrase 'every/all the towns/cities of Iran' (همه modifies شهر). کنم is the first-person singular present/future 'I make / I will make', and رود آب is a noun phrase 'a river of water' used metaphorically as the result of the action 'I will make (them) a river of water'.
segments.s223-g54: بکام دل is a prepositional/possessive phrase meaning 'to the desire/pleasure of the heart' (بکام = 'pleasing / to the desire of'). خسرو افراسیاب is the name 'Khosrow Afrasiab' (خسرو is a title/name). The whole segment gives the beneficiary or motive: 'to satisfy the heart of Khosrow Afrasiab.'
segments.s223-g55: ز (poetic form of 'از') means 'from' or 'out of'. لشکر بسی is a genitive/quantifying phrase 'a great part/number of the host/army' (بسی = many/much in classical usage). نامور گرد کرد is a verbal cluster: نامور = 'renowned', گرد (part of the classical verbal formation with کردن) + کرد = 'made' — together 'made renowned' or 'caused to become famous'.
segments.s223-g56: ز again 'from/out of'. خنجرگزاران is a compound noun 'dagger-bearers' (those who wield daggers). و is the coordinating 'and'. مردان مرد is a fixed/poetic doubling meaning 'men of men' — i.e. supreme men / true champions; مردان is plural 'men' and مرد is emphatic 'man/hero' in apposition.

sentences.s223-s17: O host, keep watch against the alert ones — anyone who would secretly seek to open speech.
sentences.s223-s18: Let them see who this lion‑hearted man is, and who it is that brought him to this mighty host.
sentences.s223-s19: From that time on we will have every person killed; we will bring them to the assembly and place their heads and bodies (there).
sentences.s223-s20: To the elders thus spoke the Khaghan of China: “The trouble itself comes from this, and the remedy/care comes from this.”
sentences.s223-s13: If it is fitting that on the day of vengeance he take the elephant's head, he lifts it up and strikes it to the ground.
sentences.s223-s14: The army, all together, came before the khagan; they became sorrowful and wept for Kamus.
sentences.s223-s15: For you have heard and seen the beginning and the end of this battlefield in the presence of the army.
sentences.s223-s16: Now seek a remedy for our affairs; confide only in your own self and tell no one.
sentences.s223-s1: Now, O wise bright‑souled one, let your tongue utter nothing but the name of God.
sentences.s223-s2: For He is the guide over good and evil, and by Him the revolving heavens are set in their place.
sentences.s223-s3: May your days pass continually upon you; may there be no dwelling for you except this peaceful one.
sentences.s223-s4: If you agree with this saying — for the dehqān has always said it since ancient times.
sentences.s223-s5: After that, news came to the Khaghan of China that Kamus had been slain on the Plain of Vengeance.
sentences.s223-s6: The Kashani, the Shagni and the troops of Balkh—on account of their Kamus—days became dark and bitter.
sentences.s223-s7: All turned their faces toward one another, for this accomplished man was quarrelsome.
sentences.s223-s8: What kind of man is he, and what is this man's name? He also related: 'Who in the world is this man?'
sentences.s223-s9: Thus Houmān said to Pīrān-šīr: “Today my life has become sated with battle.”
sentences.s223-s10: Our brave men were like the sons of Chang; so (indeed) Kamus the warrior was slain in battle.
sentences.s223-s11: In the world there was no such renown, nor was there a horseman more (like) Pīltan.
sentences.s223-s12: When Kamus-go was caught in the snare, they bound him and brought him to the assembly-place by force.
sentences.s223-s29: Thus he said: that warlike man — armed with arrows, a mounted lasso‑thrower and wearer of a dust‑cloak.
sentences.s223-s30: One must keep watch to see where his place is; search the army's left and the right flank.
sentences.s223-s31: Ask about him both in the town and about his name; after that we will settle his fate.
sentences.s223-s32: A proud, king‑worshipping horseman arrived, and he took this matter in hand.
sentences.s223-s37: First I will seek vengeance on Kāmus for him; afterwards, after his death, I will properly restore his name.
sentences.s223-s38: The Khaghan of China cried 'Go—bravo!' and (then) went before him and kissed the ground with his hand.
sentences.s223-s39: He said to him: 'If you bring back this hatred, come to me and bring your head, and you will be free of want.'
sentences.s223-s40: I will bestow on you so many gems from the treasure that afterwards you will not have to endure suffering.
sentences.s223-s33: Who was bent on fame and a seeker, brave and active in every enterprise.
sentences.s223-s34: To the khaghan thus he said: 'O proud victor, may the world be attached to you in love (may it worship you).'
sentences.s223-s35: If he is a warlike lion, I will put him lifeless; thereupon I will turn my head toward Iran.
sentences.s223-s36: Alone I, with my own body, will fight; I will bring all his name under shame.
sentences.s223-s21: How long will that harmful host remain, and where will an elephant be caught in the bend of the snare?
sentences.s223-s22: For there is no cure for one's own death; there is no way for pleading or questioning, nor any helper.
sentences.s223-s23: We were all born of the mother to death; in vain we have given our necks to it.
sentences.s223-s24: No one passes beyond the revolution of the heavens, even if an elephant were shattered upon the earth.
sentences.s223-s25: Do not let your heart be moved for him, the needy one; where was he killed — beneath the bend of the lasso?
sentences.s223-s26: Woe to the man by whom Kâmos was ruined; bind the lasso, I will lay (him) in the dust/ground.
sentences.s223-s27: I will turn every town of Iran into a river of water to satisfy the heart of Khosrow Afrasiab.
sentences.s223-s28: From the host he made many renowned; (he made famous) the dagger-bearers and the men among men.
